author: garyrong
content:
I also think Option 3 is the better approach. The additional overhead is acceptable if we also store the BALs of orphaned blocks within the weak subjectivity period.

When a deep reorg occurs, from the original pivot block P to a new pivot block P', with the common ancestor W, we can derive the required changes by collecting the mutation sets along the paths W -> P and W -> P'. By comparing these two sets, we can determine the mutations that exist in W -> P but not in W -> P'. These mutations must then be reverted.

Since the BAL does not retain the previous values of mutated states, the practical workaround is to delete those entries and re-fetch them using GetAccountRange and GetStorageRange (an empty response with proof is expected if these states are not existent). In most cases, this will likely result in a single-item range request, which is not particularly efficient from a networking perspective. However, given the constraints, this trade-off is acceptable.

There are also additional considerations, such as ensuring state consistency with an unclean shutdown while the pivot block is moving. These aspects are implementation details.

Anyway, from the protocol's perspective, we only need to improve the BAL's retention policy by storing the additional BALs of orphaned blocks, clients have the flexibility to choose the solution in case of deep reorg. It doesn't hurt even the BALs of orphaned blocks are not utilized at all.

author: nero_eth
content:
I just published an ethresear.ch post for snap v2, expanding on the rational behind snap v1, its problems and why snap v2 improves it:
https://ethresear.ch/t/snap-v2-replacing-trie-healing-with-bals/24333

Feedback welcome!
